narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 53% chance that you might not pass the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction was based on your profile: a solid 7th decile ranking in your first-year school grades, but a much lower 2nd decile in your third-year grades; a middle family income bracket; an LSAT score of 18; an undergraduate GPA of 2.70; being male; Black; and planning to attend full-time. The model weighed these factors against patterns from thousands of past students to forecast bar success. The biggest influence came from your third-year school decile, which strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar failure because it was well below the typical student's level. Next in importance was your first-year decile, which also nudged the outlook toward failure despite being above average, suggesting the model saw it as not carrying over strongly enough. Your family income followed closely, contributing further in the direction of a failure prediction compared to the dataset's average. Your LSAT score ranked fourth in influence and likewise pushed toward failure, as it was significantly lower than what most students in the data achieved. Your racial background as Black was the fifth most impactful, aligning with lower bar passage rates seen in similar profiles. Your undergraduate GPA added to the failure-leaning prediction, being below the typical 3.21 average. Being full-time played a minor role in the same direction, while your gender as male was the only feature that slightly favored a passing prediction, as males in the data passed a bit more often than average. Overall, these elements combined to tip the scales just over 50% toward failure risk. To improve your future chances, focus on rebuilding momentum in later academic stages—your drop from first to third-year performance is a red flag, so targeted study habits or tutoring could help sustain early strengths through law school and bar prep. Consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, as it's a key signal of test readiness, and seek mentorship programs that support students from similar backgrounds to navigate these patterns successfully. With deliberate steps like these, you can shift your profile toward the stronger predictors of bar passage. Keep pushing—you've already shown early potential that deserves a second chance.
